raw_case:
Perirectal angioleiomyoma preoperatively misdiagnosed as rectal cancer: a case report
Angioleiomyoma (ALM) is a rare benign perivascular (pericytic) tumor primarily composed of well-differentiated smooth muscle and vascular components. Its clinical and radiological features lack specificity, making diagnosis challenging and prone to misdiagnosis. This report summarizes the clinical data of a patient treated at our hospital who was preoperatively misdiagnosed with rectal cancer but was subsequently found to have perirectal ALM. Additionally, a review of the relevant literature is provided.

Case report

A 51-year-old female patient was admitted to the hospital, with a chief complaint of a perirectal mass noted for over 10 years. Physical examination (knee-chest position) revealed good contraction of the anal sphincter, no palpable anal canal mass at 5 cm, but a hard, extrarectal mass was palpated at 3 cm from the anal verge. There was no blood or mucus on the glove upon withdrawal. Routine blood tests, liver and kidney function tests, coagulation function tests, infectious disease series, and tumor markers were all normal.A non-contrast MR scan of the abdomen and pelvis with DWI was performed, showing a mass in the subcutaneous fat of the right buttock. The mass exhibited iso- to slightly hyperintense T1 and T2 signals, high signal on DWI, and iso- to hypointense signal on ADC ((0.95 ± 0.15)×10-3 mm2/s), with a clear boundary and a size of approximately 5.8 cm × 3.3 cm × 5.0 cm (
Figure 1
). The radiological diagnosis was a right buttock subcutaneous fat mass, and enhanced imaging was recommended.A n enhanced MR scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a mass in the subcutaneous fat of the right buttock with iso- to slightly hyperintense T1 and T2 signals. The enhanced scan showed uneven significant enhancement with patchy liquefactive necrosis within the lesion, measuring approximately 5.8 cm × 3.3 cm × 5.0 cm (
Figure 1
). The diagnostic suggestion was a subcutaneous fat mass in the right buttock, likely a malignant rectal tumor.Based on preoperative imaging, the preoperative diagnosis was a malignant rectal tumor. The patient underwent radical resection of the malignant rectal tumor under general anesthesia, with intraoperative biopsy of the right perirectal mass for rapid frozen pathology. The intraoperative frozen section revealed a spindle cell tumor of mesenchymal origin. Immunohistochemical staining indicated a high likelihood of a pericytic tumor (suggestive of myopericytoma, with stromal myxoid changes). Immunohistochemical results were: CD117 (-), Dog-1 (-), CD34 (+), S-100 (-), SOX-10 (-), SMA (+), Desmin (+), Vimentin (+), STAT-6 (-), Ki67 (+10%), HMB45 (-), MelanA (-), CK8-18 (-).The planned surgical procedure was altered, and a simple excision of the perirectal mass was performed successfully (
Figure 2
). Postoperative pathological diagnosis was: (perirectal) mesenchymal tumor, consistent with angioleiomyoma with extensive myxoid changes, based on immunohistochemistry. Immunohistochemical staining results were: CD34 (scattered +), CD31 (-, vascular +), Desmin (diffuse +), SMA (diffuse +), S-100 (-), Vimentin (+), CKP (-), Caldesmon (scattered +), TLE-1 (scattered weak +), Rb (-, loss), Muc-4 (-), Catenin-B (-) (
Figure 3
).Postoperatively, the patient received symptomatic treatments including fluid replacement, anti-infection, acid suppression, and analgesia. The patient’s condition stabilized, and she was discharged. One month later, a follow-up abdominal ultrasound showed no significant abnormalities. At the two-month follow-up, the patient was in good general condition with no signs of recurrence.

Discussion

Initially, angioleiomyoma (ALM) was considered a smooth muscle tumor. In 2013, the World Health Organization (WHO) classified it as a perivascular (pericytic) tumor. The peak incidence of ALM is between 40 and 60 years of age. Clinically, the disease often presents as a solitary, slow-growing, firm, occasionally painful cutaneous mass, primarily in the lower limbs, and predominantly occurs in females (1). Literature reports suggest that its size and pain degree may correlate with the menstrual cycle and pregnancy, indicating a potential hormone dependency (2). ALM can occur in various body parts, most commonly in the lower limbs (3), followed by the head and neck region (4), with documented cases in the intracranial and oral regions, there have been two reported cases occurring within the rectum, both of which showed favorable prognoses following simple surgical resection (5, 6), but no reported cases in the perirectal area.Regarding histogenesis, most scholars believe ALM originates from the tunica media of vein walls, while some consider it a vascular malformation (7) or an intermediate process between hemangioma and solid leiomyoma (4). The pathogenesis of ALM involves two genetic mutation patterns: the first characterized by a t(4;5)(p12;q32) translocation producing the CARMN::TXK fusion gene; the second involves recurrent Xq22 rearrangements leading to IRS4 overexpression (8). Animal studies suggest that N-nitrosoethylurea compounds may induce ALM in the spleen (9).Clinically, ALM often presents with cold-induced sudden pain, possibly due to ischemia from smooth muscle contraction induced by cold (10). However, this is not a specific symptom; for instance, this case presented no obvious symptoms, only a gradually enlarging, painless mass. Definitive diagnosis relies on postoperative histological examination and immunohistochemistry. Microscopically, ALM typically features numerous dilated blood vessels interspersed with spindle cells and collagen bundles lacking elastic fibers. The smooth muscle fibers around the vascular lumen form a regular ring pattern, and the nuclei of disordered smooth muscle cells appear cigar-shaped with eosinophilic cytoplasm. Mitosis, cytological atypia, necrosis, or pleomorphism are rarely observed. ALM can be pathologically classified into three types (11): (1) Solid/capillary type, the most common, characterized by compact smooth muscle and numerous small, slit-like vascular channels, with a higher incidence in females; (2) Venous type, featuring a thick muscle wall and loosely arranged smooth muscle bundles, more common in males; (3) Cavernous type, the rarest, composed of dilated vascular channels and a few smooth muscle bundles, also more common in males.Immunohistochemically, ALM typically shows negative staining for epithelial membrane antigen (EMA), serum acidic binding protein S100, glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), and desmin, while smooth muscle actin (SMA) and either CD34 or CD31 are markers for muscle cells and vascular endothelial cells, respectively. These specific immunohistochemical results are crucial for diagnosis and differential diagnosis (12). There have been reports of CD34-negative ALM, speculated to be due to immunohistochemical errors or an unclear CD31(+) status (13). Immunohistochemical examinations conducted in this case included DOG1, which ruled out a diagnosis of GIST, and S-100 protein, which excluded a diagnosis of peripheral nerve sheath tumor.In imaging, MRI has diagnostic value for ALM. ALM lesions show homogeneous low signal intensity similar to skeletal muscle on T1-weighted images. Solid and venous types exhibit heterogeneous signal intensity on T2-weighted images, appearing isointense or slightly hyperintense, while cavernous types show high signal intensity. Gadolinium-enhanced scans show heterogeneous enhancement (13). Some researchers suggest that a mixed high and isointense signal on T2-weighted MRI with a low signal margin should prompt a diagnosis of angioleiomyoma (14). However, in this case, MRI revealed that the mass exhibited high T1 and T2 signal intensity, which contrasts significantly with previous reports in the literature. This serves as a reminder that during the diagnostic process for such conditions, multiple diagnostic methods, particularly pathology, should be utilized to make a well-informed decision. On ultrasound, ALM typically appears as an oval, well-defined, homogeneous hypoechoic mass. Gray-scale ultrasound shows low echogenicity in the venous type and isoechoic in the solid type. Color Doppler imaging shows rich blood flow in the venous type and relatively poor flow in the solid type, corresponding to their microscopic appearance, thus having value in differentiating ALM subtypes (15). Preoperative CT diagnosis of ALM is extremely challenging, showing irregular, heterogeneous density masses with uneven nodular enhancement (16). Despite the atypical appearance, CT imaging remains indispensable due to its detailed, realistic, and three-dimensional reconstruction capabilities, aiding in precise elective surgical excision and reducing surgical trauma and complications.For treatment and prognosis, simple excision is the preferred treatment for ALM. Surgical excision alleviates clinical symptoms and, given the high misdiagnosis rate, allows for accurate pathological examination to confirm the diagnosis. There have been cases where angioleiomyoma progressed to angioleiomyosarcoma, indicating that delayed or inappropriate treatment increases the risk of malignancy (17). Therefore, timely and active treatment is necessary. The prognosis is excellent postoperatively, with an extremely low recurrence rate, and no human ALM recurrence cases have been reported. Annual follow-up is recommended after excision (18).In conclusion, ALM is a rare benign tumor, and its occurrence in the perirectal area is exceedingly rare. Complete excision of the lesion is the preferred treatment for all types, with excellent surgical outcomes. Despite the difficulty in preoperative diagnosis, clinicians must conduct thorough preoperative evaluations and examinations to avoid misdiagnosis and restore patient health with minimal cost.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 51-year-old female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Perirectal mass noted for over 10 years.
- **History of Present Illness:** Gradually enlarging, painless mass over a decade.
- **Past Medical History:** None provided.
- **Personal History:** None provided.
- **Family History:** None provided.
- **Physical Examination:** Hard, extrarectal mass palpated 3 cm from the anal verge; no anal canal mass; good anal sphincter contraction. No evidence of blood or mucus.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Routine blood, liver/kidney function, coagulation, tumor markers: Normal.
  - MRI: Subcutaneous fat mass in the right buttock (5.8 cm × 3.3 cm × 5.0 cm), with iso- to hyperintense T1/T2 signals, high DWI signal, iso- to hypointense ADC signal ((0.95 ± 0.15) × 10^-3 mm²/s), uneven significant enhancement, and patchy necrosis. Radiological diagnosis suggested malignancy (likely a malignant rectal tumor).
  - Intraoperative frozen pathology: Spindle cell tumor of mesenchymal origin; immunohistochemistry indicated a high likelihood of pericytic tumor.
  - Immunohistochemical results: Positive for CD34, SMA, Desmin, Vimentin; negative for CD117, Dog-1, S-100, SOX-10, HMB45, and others.
  - Final postoperative pathological diagnosis: Perirectal angioleiomyoma with extensive myxoid changes.
- **Treatment:** Radical resection of the mass; subsequent symptomatic and supportive postoperative care.
- **Follow-Up:** No abnormalities on abdominal ultrasound at one month; no signs of recurrence at two months.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Consideration:** Based on the chief complaint of a long-standing perirectal mass, the primary suspicion included rectal malignancy due to imaging findings (irregular enhancing lesion with necrosis). 
   
2. **Consideration of Rectal Cancer:**
   - **Rationale for Consideration:** Common malignant entity with overlapping clinical and imaging features, such as a enlarging mass potentially invading perirectal tissues.
   - **Exclusion Reason:** Intraoperative frozen pathology revealed a spindle cell tumor with likely mesenchymal origin. Immunohistochemical results ruled out common malignancies such as gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST, DOG1-negative) and rectal carcinoma (CK8-18-negative).

3. **Consideration of Peripheral Nerve Sheath Tumor:**
   - **Rationale for Consideration:** Benign spindle cell tumors may appear similar histologically.
   - **Exclusion Reason:** S-100 and SOX-10 negativity excluded this.

4. **Final Diagnosis of Angioleiomyoma:** 
   - **Rationale for Confirmation:** Histopathological findings of spindle-shaped smooth muscle cells with vascular components, immunohistochemistry (Desmin+, SMA+, Vimentin+, with scattered CD34+), and absence of malignancy markers confirmed the benign nature of the tumor, identifying it as a perivascular tumor (angioleiomyoma). 

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The final diagnosis is *perirectal angioleiomyoma (ALM)*, a rare benign perivascular tumor. Supported by histopathology demonstrating smooth muscle and vascular components with specific immunohistochemical markers (e.g., SMA, Desmin) and lack of typical malignant features. Initial misdiagnosis of malignancy was corrected intraoperatively based on frozen section and subsequent complete histopathological evaluation.

---

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Summarize Key Clinical Findings:** A 51-year-old female presented with a long-standing (over 10 years), gradually enlarging, painless perirectal mass. Physical examination revealed a hard, extrarectal mass without anal canal involvement or bleeding/mucus. Anal sphincter function was normal.

<step 2> **Analyze Radiological Findings (MRI):**  MRI showed a subcutaneous fat mass in the right buttock with imaging characteristics suggestive of malignancy. Specifically, the size (5.8 cm × 3.3 cm × 5.0 cm), signal intensities (iso- to hyperintense T1/T2, high DWI, iso- to hypointense ADC), uneven enhancement, and patchy necrosis raised suspicion for a malignant rectal tumor, despite being described as subcutaneous.  This highlights the limitations of imaging alone in differentiating benign from malignant masses, especially in soft tissues.

<step 3> **Interpret Intraoperative Frozen Pathology:**  The frozen section identified a spindle cell tumor of mesenchymal origin and suggested a pericytic tumor. This was crucial in shifting the differential diagnosis away from typical rectal carcinomas (epithelial origin) and towards mesenchymal neoplasms.  Spindle cell tumors are a broad category, but mesenchymal origin directs focus to tumors of connective tissue, muscle, blood vessels, etc.

<step 4> **Evaluate Immunohistochemical Results:** Immunohistochemistry provided a more specific profile. Positivity for CD34, SMA (Smooth Muscle Actin), Desmin, and Vimentin strongly supports a mesenchymal lesion with smooth muscle differentiation.  The negativity for CD117, Dog-1, S-100, SOX-10, HMB45 effectively rules out several other mesenchymal tumors like GIST (CD117, Dog-1), nerve sheath tumors (S-100, SOX-10), and melanoma (HMB45).  This immunohistochemical profile is highly consistent with a smooth muscle tumor, particularly angioleiomyoma.

<step 5> **Confirm Final Diagnosis with Postoperative Pathology:** The final postoperative pathological diagnosis of "Perirectal angioleiomyoma with extensive myxoid changes" definitively establishes the nature of the mass. Angioleiomyoma is a benign smooth muscle tumor often associated with blood vessels. Myxoid changes are a degenerative process where the tissue becomes gelatinous due to accumulation of mucopolysaccharides. This explains the MRI findings which can sometimes mimic malignancy due to the heterogeneity and enhancement patterns associated with myxoid changes and vascularity.

<step 6> **Integrate Clinical, Radiological and Pathological Findings:** The clinical presentation (slow-growing, painless mass) is somewhat atypical for a rapidly progressing malignancy, but the long duration and size made investigation necessary.  The MRI findings were misleading towards malignancy, highlighting the importance of pathological diagnosis.  The frozen section and subsequent immunohistochemistry were pivotal in guiding towards the correct diagnostic category. The final pathology confirms a benign entity, angioleiomyoma, explaining the clinical course and resolving the initial concerns raised by imaging. The follow-up data (no recurrence at two months) further supports the benign nature and successful resection.

<step 7> **Final Diagnostic Conclusion:** Based on the comprehensive analysis of clinical presentation, radiological findings, and most importantly, the definitive pathological diagnosis (including immunohistochemistry), the final diagnosis is unequivocally Perirectal Angioleiomyoma with extensive myxoid changes. The initial radiological suspicion of malignancy was not borne out by pathology, demonstrating the crucial role of histopathological examination in definitive diagnosis.

### Answer:
Perirectal Angioleiomyoma with extensive myxoid changes.

reasoning_eval.step: **Summarize Key Clinical Findings:** A 51-year-old female presented with a long-standing (over 10 years), gradually enlarging, painless perirectal mass. Physical examination revealed a hard, extrarectal mass without anal canal involvement or bleeding/mucus. Anal sphincter function was normal.

**Analyze Radiological Findings (MRI):**  MRI showed a subcutaneous fat mass in the right buttock with imaging characteristics suggestive of malignancy. Specifically, the size (5.8 cm × 3.3 cm × 5.0 cm), signal intensities (iso- to hyperintense T1/T2, high DWI, iso- to hypointense ADC), uneven enhancement, and patchy necrosis raised suspicion for a malignant rectal tumor, despite being described as subcutaneous.  This highlights the limitations of imaging alone in differentiating benign from malignant masses, especially in soft tissues.

**Interpret Intraoperative Frozen Pathology:**  The frozen section identified a spindle cell tumor of mesenchymal origin and suggested a pericytic tumor. This was crucial in shifting the differential diagnosis away from typical rectal carcinomas (epithelial origin) and towards mesenchymal neoplasms.  Spindle cell tumors are a broad category, but mesenchymal origin directs focus to tumors of connective tissue, muscle, blood vessels, etc.

**Evaluate Immunohistochemical Results:** Immunohistochemistry provided a more specific profile. Positivity for CD34, SMA (Smooth Muscle Actin), Desmin, and Vimentin strongly supports a mesenchymal lesion with smooth muscle differentiation.  The negativity for CD117, Dog-1, S-100, SOX-10, HMB45 effectively rules out several other mesenchymal tumors like GIST (CD117, Dog-1), nerve sheath tumors (S-100, SOX-10), and melanoma (HMB45).  This immunohistochemical profile is highly consistent with a smooth muscle tumor, particularly angioleiomyoma.

**Confirm Final Diagnosis with Postoperative Pathology:** The final postoperative pathological diagnosis of "Perirectal angioleiomyoma with extensive myxoid changes" definitively establishes the nature of the mass. Angioleiomyoma is a benign smooth muscle tumor often associated with blood vessels. Myxoid changes are a degenerative process where the tissue becomes gelatinous due to accumulation of mucopolysaccharides. This explains the MRI findings which can sometimes mimic malignancy due to the heterogeneity and enhancement patterns associated with myxoid changes and vascularity.

**Integrate Clinical, Radiological and Pathological Findings:** The clinical presentation (slow-growing, painless mass) is somewhat atypical for a rapidly progressing malignancy, but the long duration and size made investigation necessary.  The MRI findings were misleading towards malignancy, highlighting the importance of pathological diagnosis.  The frozen section and subsequent immunohistochemistry were pivotal in guiding towards the correct diagnostic category. The final pathology confirms a benign entity, angioleiomyoma, explaining the clinical course and resolving the initial concerns raised by imaging. The follow-up data (no recurrence at two months) further supports the benign nature and successful resection.

**Final Diagnostic Conclusion:** Based on the comprehensive analysis of clinical presentation, radiological findings, and most importantly, the definitive pathological diagnosis (including immunohistochemistry), the final diagnosis is unequivocally Perirectal Angioleiomyoma with extensive myxoid changes. The initial radiological suspicion of malignancy was not borne out by pathology, demonstrating the crucial role of histopathological examination in definitive diagnosis.

<Step 2> Rationale for considering rectal cancer as a common malignant entity with overlapping clinical and imaging features, such as an enlarging mass potentially invading perirectal tissues.

<Step 3> Intraoperative frozen pathology revealed a spindle cell tumor with a likely mesenchymal origin, ruling out gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST, DOG1-negative) and rectal carcinoma (CK8-18-negative).
<Step 4> Benign spindle cell tumors may appear similar histologically, leading to the consideration of peripheral nerve sheath tumors.

<Step 6> Histopathological findings of spindle-shaped smooth muscle cells with vascular components, along with specific immunohistochemical markers (Desmin+, SMA+, Vimentin+, with scattered CD34+) confirmed the benign nature of the tumor.
<Step 7> The tumor was identified as a perivascular tumor (angioleiomyoma) based on the histopathological and immunohistochemical findings.
<Step 8> The final diagnosis is perirectal angioleiomyoma (ALM), a rare benign perivascular tumor.
<Step 9> The initial misdiagnosis of malignancy was corrected intraoperatively based on frozen section and subsequent complete histopathological evaluation.
